moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the king's pawn, staking our claim to the center and preparing for rapid development. This move controls the vital d5 and f5 squares while opening diagonals for our bishop and queen. Against the Pirc Defense, we're setting up a classical center that Black will challenge in their own unique way - not immediately contesting the center with pawns, but preparing to undermine it with pieces and later pawn breaks. Our plan is to build a strong pawn center and launch a kingside attack, which is exactly what the Pirc often invites.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1.e4 claims the center, controls d5 and f5, and opens lines for the queen and bishop against the Pirc Defense.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black chooses the Pirc Defense, a hypermodern opening that allows us to build a big center while preparing to strike back later. This move supports an eventual ...e5 or ...c5 pawn break and keeps options open for piece development. Instead of immediately challenging our e4 pawn, Black is following the philosophy that a premature central confrontation might favor White's superior space and development. We should be prepared for Black to fianchetto the king's pawn on g7 and create long-term pressure against our center.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's Pirc Defense fianchettoes the king's pawn on g7 to pressure White's big center later.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We complete our ideal pawn center, controlling the crucial e5 and c5 squares while gaining maximum space advantage. This move opens the c1-h6 diagonal for our light-squared bishop and gives us excellent piece coordination possibilities. The d4 pawn works together with e4 to create what many call the 'classical center' - two pawns abreast controlling key squares. Against the Pirc, this setup is particularly strong because Black hasn't yet created immediate counterplay, giving us time to develop our pieces optimally and prepare an attack.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: d4 completes the classical pawn center, controlling e5 and c5 while opening the c1-h6 diagonal for the bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to its ideal square, attacking our e4 pawn and preparing to support the center from the flank. This knight will often be a key defender in Black's setup, and from f6 it can potentially jump to d7, h5, or even g4 in some lines. Our opponent is following typical Pirc principles - developing pieces before committing to pawn structures and maintaining flexibility. We need to be ready to defend e4, and this is exactly why moves like Nc3 or f3 are common continuations in these positions.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight on f6 attacks e4 and can jump to d7, h5, or g4 — defend with Nc3 or f3.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop our knight to its best square, defending the e4 pawn while increasing our control over the central d5 square. This knight placement is incredibly flexible - it can support advances with f4-f5, help in a potential e5 pawn break, or even redeploy to e2-g3 in some attacking formations. The knight also eyes the b5 and d5 outposts, which could become important in the middlegame. This is a purposeful move that maintains all our options while solidifying our central control.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight to f3 defends e4, eyes d5, and keeps flexible options for f5, e5, or g3 redeployment.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black begins the characteristic Pirc fianchetto, preparing to place the dark-squared bishop on g7 where it will exert long-range pressure against our center. This move also prepares kingside castling and creates potential counterplay along the long diagonal. Our opponent is now clearly committed to the Pirc setup, which means we can start planning our typical attacking formations. The g6 move slightly weakens the kingside dark squares, particularly f6 and h6, which we might be able to exploit later with moves like Qd2 and Bh6.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's g6 fianchetto weakens f6 and h6, setting up potential Qd2 and Bh6 to exploit those dark squares.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop our dark-squared bishop to an active square, supporting the d4 pawn while preparing potential central expansion with f4-f5. This bishop placement is part of the '150 Attack' system, where we aim for quick development and kingside aggression. The bishop on e3 also eyes the a7-g1 diagonal and can support various pawn advances or piece maneuvers. This move keeps maximum flexibility while maintaining our space advantage and preparing for more aggressive continuations.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bishop to e3 supports d4, eyes the a7-g1 diagonal, and prepares f4-f5 for kingside aggression.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays a well-grounded move that supports the center and prepares potential expansion with ...b5 or ...d5. This move also controls the important d5 and b5 squares, limiting some of our piece placements and central advances. Our opponent is showing flexibility - the c6 pawn can support ...d5 at the right moment or help create queenside counterplay with ...b5-b4. However, this move doesn't directly address our growing kingside attacking potential, which means we should continue with our aggressive development and look for ways to intensify the pressure.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's c6 supports ...d5 or ...b5 but ignores your kingside attack — continue aggressive development.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the signature move of the Sveshnikov-Jansa Attack variation, creating a secure square for our dark-squared bishop on g4 while preparing aggressive kingside expansion. This move prevents Black from developing the light-squared bishop to g4, where it would pin our knight and disrupt our coordination. The h3 square also gives us the option to play g4-g5 later, and our bishop can retreat to h2 if needed, maintaining pressure on the long diagonal. While this might look slow, it's actually preparing a concrete attacking plan and is much more forcing than it appears - we're setting up Qd2, castling queenside, and launching a pawn storm with f4-f5 and g4-g5.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: h3 secures g4 for the dark-squared bishop, prevents Black's bishop pinning the knight, and prepares queenside castling with a kingside pawn storm.
